clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the outpatient gynaecology clinic with a 4-day history of lower abdominal pain, mucopurulent cervical discharge, and cervical motion tenderness on bimanual examination. Her temperature is 37.6°C, pulse 84 bpm. Pregnancy test is negative. NAAT for chlamydia and gonorrhoea is pending. She has no allergy history and is not systemically unwell. Which antibiotic regimen is the most appropriate outpatient treatment?
options: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
Intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg single dose plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg three times daily for 7 days
Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose as monotherapy
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
explanation: Current guidelines (BASHH/CDC) recommend IM ceftriaxone (to cover Neisseria gonorrhoeae) plus 14 days of oral doxycycline (to cover Chlamydia trachomatis and other organisms) plus metronidazole (to cover anaerobes, including those implicated in bacterial vaginosis-associated PID). Monotherapy with doxycycline, azithromycin, or a fluoroquinolone alone does not provide adequate polymicrobial coverage. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is not a standard PID regimen.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Doxycycline monotherapy lacks adequate coverage for gonorrhoea and anaerobes; combination therapy is mandatory.
Correct — this triple regimen provides broad-spectrum coverage against the polymicrobial aetiology of PID including gonorrhoea, chlamydia, and anaerobes.
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is not a recommended regimen for PID; it does not reliably cover chlamydia or provide the combination breadth needed.
Azithromycin 1 g as a single dose is used for uncomplicated chlamydial cervicitis, not PID, which requires extended multi-drug coverage.
Fluoroquinolone resistance in gonorrhoea is widespread; ciprofloxacin monotherapy is no longer recommended for PID by current guidelines.
clinicalPearl: Outpatient PID treatment requires at least three drugs: a cephalosporin for gonorrhoea, doxycycline for chlamydia, and metronidazole for anaerobes — all for 14 days.
keyClue: Cervical motion tenderness with mucopurulent discharge in a sexually active young woman indicates PID requiring combination antimicrobial therapy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing doxycycline alone — students forget gonorrhoea and anaerobic coverage are also required.
missedConcept: PID is polymicrobial; monotherapy never meets guideline-recommended coverage.

subtopic: When to hospitalise and IV antibiotics
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her partner with a 2-day history of severe lower abdominal pain, vomiting, and high fever. She is unable to tolerate oral intake. Examination reveals temperature 39.2°C, pulse 112 bpm, BP 98/64 mmHg, marked bilateral lower abdominal tenderness, rebound tenderness, and cervical excitation. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm tubo-ovarian complex on the right. Pregnancy test is negative. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Discharge with oral ceftriaxone, doxycycline, and metronidazole for 14 days
Admit for intravenous cefoxitin plus doxycycline, with reassessment at 72 hours for surgical drainage if not improving
Arrange urgent laparoscopy as first-line treatment without antibiotic trial
Commence oral doxycycline and metronidazole and reassess in 48 hours as outpatient
Prescribe oral co-amoxiclav and arrange pelvic MRI within one week
explanation: This patient has tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) with systemic sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, vomiting) — clear indications for hospital admission and intravenous antibiotics. IV cefoxitin plus doxycycline (or an equivalent broad-spectrum IV regimen) is the standard inpatient PID/TOA regimen. Most TOAs respond to antibiotics alone; surgical or radiological drainage is reserved for those failing 72 hours of IV therapy or rupture.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Outpatient oral antibiotics are inappropriate when the patient is systemically unwell, cannot tolerate oral intake, and has a tubo-ovarian abscess.
Correct — inpatient IV antibiotics with close monitoring and planned reassessment for drainage if no improvement is guideline-concordant management.
Laparoscopy is not first-line for TOA; antibiotics are tried first unless rupture, haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation, or no response at 72 hours.
Outpatient management is contraindicated in this hemodynamically compromised patient who cannot tolerate oral medications.
Co-amoxiclav is not a standard PID IV regimen and delaying imaging does not address the acute septic state requiring immediate IV treatment.
clinicalPearl: Tubo-ovarian abscess with systemic sepsis mandates hospital admission and IV antibiotics; surgical drainage is only required if there is no clinical improvement after 72 hours.
keyClue: Fever 39.2°C, hypotension, vomiting, and a tubo-ovarian complex on ultrasound are absolute indications for inpatient IV therapy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing immediate surgery — TOA is primarily managed with antibiotics; surgery is a last resort.
missedConcept: Criteria for inpatient PID management include tubo-ovarian abscess, systemic sepsis, failure of outpatient therapy, and inability to tolerate oral medications.
redFlagReason: Hypotension (BP 98/64) and tachycardia indicate septic shock requiring immediate resuscitation and inpatient IV antibiotics; delayed treatment risks tubo-ovarian abscess rupture.

clinicalStem: A 20-year-old woman is diagnosed with chlamydial PID and treated with the appropriate antibiotic regimen. She has had two sexual partners in the past three months. She asks what else should be done to prevent recurrence. In addition to completing her course of antibiotics, what is the most important preventive measure?
options: Recommend she use an intrauterine device as contraception to prevent reinfection
Advise her to avoid intercourse permanently
Ensure all sexual partners from the past 60 days are tested and treated, and advise abstinence or condom use until both she and her partners have completed treatment
Prescribe 6 months of prophylactic doxycycline after treatment completion
Arrange annual CA-125 measurement to screen for subsequent pelvic disease
explanation: Partner notification, testing, and treatment is the cornerstone of STI/PID prevention; failure to treat partners leads to reinfection (ping-pong infection). Guidelines recommend tracing partners from the past 60 days. Abstinence or barrier contraception until treatment is completed in all partners prevents transmission. Prophylactic long-term antibiotics are not indicated, and an IUD does not prevent reinfection.
wrongAnswerExplanations: An IUD does not prevent reinfection with STIs; in fact, IUD insertion in the context of untreated PID is contraindicated.
Permanent abstinence is not a realistic or recommended public health measure; partner treatment and barrier contraception are the practical intervention.
Correct — partner notification and treatment is the most important preventive step to prevent reinfection and ongoing transmission.
Long-term prophylactic doxycycline is not indicated after successful PID treatment; the focus is on partner management and barrier contraception.
CA-125 is a tumour marker with no role in STI follow-up or PID surveillance.
clinicalPearl: Without partner notification and treatment, up to 30% of women treated for chlamydial PID are reinfected within months — making partner management as important as the antibiotic choice.
keyClue: Two sexual partners in the past 3 months — all contacts within 60 days must be traced, tested, and treated.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on the patient's antibiotics and overlooking partner notification, the crucial public health intervention.
missedConcept: Contact tracing is mandatory in STI-associated PID; treating only the index case leads to reinfection.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman at 39 weeks gestation presents in active labour. Her antenatal records show a positive rectovaginal Group B Streptococcus (GBS) swab at 36 weeks. She reports a previous reaction to penicillin consisting of urticaria and throat swelling within 30 minutes of administration. What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis?
options: Intravenous ampicillin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 4 hours
Intravenous clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours, after confirming susceptibility of the GBS isolate
Oral amoxicillin 500 mg every 8 hours throughout labour
Intravenous cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours
No antibiotic prophylaxis; arrange neonatal observation only
explanation: This patient has a high-risk penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis-type: urticaria plus angioedema within 30 minutes), contraindicating penicillin and cephalosporins due to cross-reactivity risk. In high-risk penicillin allergy with a GBS isolate susceptible to clindamycin, IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours is the recommended alternative. The isolate's susceptibility must be confirmed beforehand, as clindamycin-resistant GBS strains are common. If resistant or susceptibility unknown, vancomycin is used.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ampicillin is a penicillin and is absolutely contraindicated in this high-risk penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis within 30 minutes).
Correct — IV clindamycin with confirmed susceptibility is the guideline-recommended alternative for high-risk penicillin allergy; vancomycin is used if resistant.
Oral antibiotics are not used for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis; adequate tissue and neonatal levels require intravenous administration.
Cefazolin is the first alternative for low-risk penicillin allergy (rash only, no anaphylaxis); it is not safe for anaphylaxis-type reactions due to cross-reactivity risk.
Withholding prophylaxis in a GBS-positive patient with known allergy without an alternative is not appropriate; clindamycin or vancomycin must be used.
clinicalPearl: In high-risk penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis, urticaria with angioedema), cefazolin is unsafe; clindamycin (susceptibility-confirmed) or vancomycin is used for GBS intrapartum prophylaxis.
keyClue: Urticaria plus throat swelling within 30 minutes = high-risk IgE-mediated penicillin allergy, ruling out all beta-lactams including cephalosporins.
commonExamTrap: Using cefazolin in penicillin anaphylaxis — safe only for mild (low-risk) penicillin reactions.
missedConcept: Penicillin allergy risk stratification determines the GBS prophylaxis alternative: cefazolin for low-risk; clindamycin or vancomycin for high-risk.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old primigravida attends her first antenatal booking appointment at 10 weeks gestation. She has no complaints and her examination is unremarkable. Her antenatal blood results return positive for syphilis serology (RPR reactive, TPHA confirmed). She has not been previously treated. What is the most appropriate management to prevent congenital syphilis?
options: Defer treatment until after delivery to avoid teratogenicity
Administer benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM in a single dose and arrange follow-up serology
Prescribe oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 28 days
Reassure and repeat serology in the third trimester before deciding on treatment
Refer for termination of pregnancy as the foetus is already infected
explanation: Benzathine penicillin G is the only antibiotic proven to treat maternal syphilis and prevent congenital syphilis; it crosses the placenta adequately. Early stage syphilis is treated with a single IM injection of 2.4 MU; treatment should not be deferred — earlier treatment in pregnancy gives better outcomes. Follow-up RPR titres at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months monitor treatment response.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Deferring treatment risks vertical transmission leading to congenital syphilis, stillbirth, hydrops, and neonatal death; treatment in pregnancy is urgent.
Correct — benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM is the WHO/BASHH first-line regimen for syphilis in pregnancy; it prevents congenital infection.
Doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy; it causes dental staining and bone dysplasia and is not reliable against T. pallidum in the foetus.
Repeating serology and delaying treatment risks ongoing foetal infection; treatment should begin as soon as diagnosis is confirmed.
Early-stage maternal syphilis treated promptly with penicillin has high rates of preventing congenital syphilis; termination is not indicated.
clinicalPearl: Benzathine penicillin G is the only recommended treatment for syphilis in pregnancy; doxycycline is contraindicated and does not reliably prevent congenital infection.
keyClue: Positive syphilis serology at booking in a previously untreated woman — immediate treatment with benzathine penicillin G prevents vertical transmission.
commonExamTrap: Selecting doxycycline as the penicillin allergy alternative — doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy.
missedConcept: Doxycycline, the usual penicillin alternative for syphilis, is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy; desensitisation to penicillin should be considered instead.

subtopic: Antiviral prophylaxis in primary genital HSV near term
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with her first-ever episode of painful genital ulceration. HSV-2 PCR is positive. She had no history of genital herpes prior to this pregnancy. She is currently experiencing active lesions. What is the most appropriate antiviral management to reduce the risk of neonatal herpes?
options: No treatment; reassure her that neonatal HSV is rare and proceed with planned vaginal delivery
Commence oral aciclovir 400 mg three times daily from 36 weeks until delivery, and plan elective caesarean section if active lesions are present at onset of labour
Administer a single dose of IV aciclovir now and repeat only if she develops recurrence at term
Start oral valaciclovir 500 mg daily as suppressive therapy; vaginal delivery is safe regardless of lesion status
Prescribe topical aciclovir cream to the lesions and reassess at 38 weeks
explanation: Primary genital HSV in the third trimester carries the highest risk of neonatal herpes (up to 40-50%) because of high viral shedding and lack of maternal antibodies to transfer. RCOG/BASHH guidelines recommend oral aciclovir 400 mg TDS from 36 weeks to suppress viral shedding, combined with elective caesarean section if active lesions or prodrome is present at onset of labour. Topical aciclovir alone is ineffective for systemic viral suppression.
wrongAnswerExplanations: No treatment for primary HSV at 36 weeks is dangerous; neonatal HSV from primary maternal infection has up to 50% transmission risk during vaginal delivery.
Correct — oral aciclovir suppressive therapy from 36 weeks and caesarean section for active lesions at delivery is the evidence-based management for primary HSV near term.
A single IV dose is not suppressive therapy; continuous oral aciclovir from 36 weeks is required to reduce viral shedding until delivery.
Vaginal delivery is NOT safe if active lesions are present at onset of labour — caesarean section is recommended in this setting to reduce neonatal transmission.
Topical aciclovir cream does not achieve systemic antiviral effect and does not reduce neonatal transmission risk.
clinicalPearl: Primary genital HSV at 36 weeks requires oral aciclovir suppression from 36 weeks and caesarean section if active lesions are present at labour onset — neonatal transmission risk from primary infection is up to 50%.
keyClue: First-ever episode of genital HSV confirmed at 36 weeks — this is primary infection near term, the highest-risk scenario for neonatal herpes.
commonExamTrap: Thinking vaginal delivery is always safe with suppressive aciclovir — caesarean section is still required if active lesions are present at onset of labour.
missedConcept: Primary HSV carries a much higher neonatal transmission risk than recurrent HSV; the management is more aggressive, including surgical delivery.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with HIV (CD4 count 420 cells/μL, viral load 18,000 copies/mL) at 14 weeks gestation. She is antiretroviral-naive. She is keen to breastfeed. What combination of interventions most effectively reduces the risk of mother-to-child HIV transmission?
options: Start antiretroviral therapy now, aim for undetectable viral load by 36 weeks, plan elective caesarean section only if viral load remains detectable, and advise formula feeding
Defer antiretroviral therapy until after delivery to avoid teratogenicity in the first trimester
Provide single-dose nevirapine to the mother at onset of labour only
Start antiretroviral therapy, plan vaginal delivery regardless of viral load, and permit breastfeeding with infant prophylaxis
Commence zidovudine monotherapy and plan elective caesarean section at 38 weeks
explanation: Starting combination ART immediately (regardless of CD4 count) to achieve undetectable viral load (<50 copies/mL) by 36 weeks is the cornerstone of PMTCT. If viral load is undetectable at 36 weeks, vaginal delivery can be considered. Caesarean section is recommended if viral load remains detectable (>400 copies/mL). Formula feeding is recommended in the UK and high-income settings to eliminate postnatal transmission through breast milk, regardless of viral load.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — early combination ART, viral load monitoring with mode of delivery guided by VL at 36 weeks, and formula feeding are the complete PMTCT package.
Deferring ART during pregnancy is dangerous; combination ART is started immediately regardless of gestational age to reduce viral load and protect the foetus.
Single-dose nevirapine alone is an outdated inadequate regimen; combination ART is now standard of care with transmission rates below 1% when used correctly.

Breastfeeding in high-income settings is not recommended for HIV-positive mothers even with undetectable viral load, as no amount of breast milk is considered completely safe; formula feeding eliminates this route.
Zidovudine monotherapy is obsolete; it does not adequately suppress viral load and is no longer recommended in current guidelines.
clinicalPearl: PMTCT requires combination ART started early in pregnancy to achieve undetectable viral load by 36 weeks, mode of delivery guided by viral load, and avoidance of breastfeeding in high-income settings.
keyClue: Detectable viral load at 14 weeks requires immediate combination ART initiation; the goal is VL <50 copies/mL before delivery.
commonExamTrap: Permitting breastfeeding because viral load is undetectable — in the UK/high-income setting, formula feeding is universally recommended for HIV-positive mothers.
missedConcept: Three pillars of PMTCT: maternal ART (viral suppression), mode of delivery guided by viral load, and avoiding breastfeeding.

clinicalStem: A 25-year-old primigravida undergoes an instrumental vaginal delivery with Kjelland's forceps and sustains a 3b perineal laceration (more than 50% of the external anal sphincter torn). The repair is performed in theatre under regional anaesthesia by an experienced surgeon. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic management immediately following repair?
options: No antibiotics are required as the wound was repaired under sterile conditions
A single dose of co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV at the time of repair, followed by oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg three times daily for 7 days
Topical chlorhexidine spray to the wound for 5 days only
Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 5 days only
IV cefuroxime 750 mg single dose intraoperatively with no further antibiotics
explanation: RCOG guidance (Green-top Guideline No. 29) recommends intraoperative broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV) at the time of repair for all third- and fourth-degree perineal tears, followed by a 7-day course of oral broad-spectrum antibiotics, to reduce the risk of wound breakdown, perineal sepsis, and recto-vaginal fistula formation. Third-degree tears involve the anal sphincter and carry significant risk of anaerobic contamination from proximity to the anorectal canal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: No antibiotics is incorrect; RCOG guidelines mandate prophylactic antibiotics for all third- and fourth-degree tears regardless of surgical technique, due to risk of faecal contamination and sphincter breakdown.
Correct — intraoperative IV co-amoxiclav plus a 7-day oral course is the RCOG-recommended regimen for obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASI).
Topical antiseptics alone do not provide systemic coverage for the deep sphincter repair and risk of faecal contamination.
Metronidazole monotherapy covers anaerobes but not aerobic Gram-negative organisms; broad-spectrum coverage is required for OASI.
A single intraoperative dose of cefuroxime is inadequate; RCOG recommends a complete 7-day oral course post-repair for OASI.
clinicalPearl: All obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASI — third and fourth degree tears) require intraoperative IV antibiotics plus a 7-day oral broad-spectrum course to prevent wound infection and sphincter breakdown.
keyClue: 3b perineal laceration involving >50% of the external anal sphincter = OASI, mandating antibiotic prophylaxis per RCOG guidelines.
commonExamTrap: Omitting post-operative oral antibiotics and giving only intraoperative prophylaxis, which is insufficient for OASI.
missedConcept: OASI antibiotic prophylaxis must cover both aerobes and anaerobes for 7 days postoperatively due to proximity to the anorectal canal.

clinicalStem: A 27-year-old woman returns to the postnatal ward on day 3 following a primary repair of a second-degree perineal laceration. She is afebrile, complains of moderate perineal discomfort, and on examination the wound is intact with mild peri-wound erythema but no discharge or dehiscence. She asks what she should do to prevent wound breakdown and speed healing. Which of the following is the most evidence-based preventive recommendation?
options: Apply topical oestrogen cream twice daily to promote tissue healing
Take daily salt baths (sitz baths), keep the area clean and dry, apply ice packs for the first 24 hours, and complete any prescribed antibiotics
Apply a tight perineal pad constantly to provide compression and prevent dehiscence
Begin pelvic floor exercises immediately on day 1 to restore tissue perfusion
Prescribe oral iron supplementation only, as anaemia is the primary cause of poor wound healing postpartum
explanation: Evidence-based perineal wound care after repair includes: keeping the wound clean and dry, regular sitz baths to promote hygiene and comfort, ice packs in the first 24 hours to reduce oedema and haematoma, adequate analgesia, and completing prescribed antibiotics. Tight perineal pads increase moisture and maceration. Topical oestrogen has no evidence base for acute perineal wound healing. Pelvic floor exercises should be started gently after wound healing, not on day 1.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical oestrogen cream has no established evidence base for enhancing healing of acute perineal lacerations and is not routinely recommended.
Correct — sitz baths, keeping the wound clean and dry, ice in the first 24 hours, and completing antibiotics are evidence-based measures to prevent wound breakdown.
Tight constant compression with a perineal pad increases moisture and maceration, promoting bacterial growth and wound breakdown rather than preventing it.
Pelvic floor exercises are recommended postpartum but should be gentle and introduced gradually; aggressive early initiation does not prevent wound breakdown.
Iron supplementation addresses anaemia but is not the primary intervention for local wound care; adequate hygiene and moisture management are more directly preventive.
clinicalPearl: Perineal wound care: sitz baths, keeping dry, ice in the first 24 hours, and completing antibiotics reduce breakdown risk — tight pads and moisture increase it.
keyClue: Mild erythema on day 3 is an early warning — evidence-based wound hygiene measures at this point can prevent full dehiscence.
commonExamTrap: Recommending tight perineal compression as protective — it actually promotes maceration and infection.
missedConcept: Perineal wound hygiene (dry, clean, sitz baths) is as important as antibiotics in preventing breakdown; moisture is the enemy.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman is on the postnatal ward on day 2 following an emergency caesarean section for prolonged rupture of membranes. She develops a temperature of 38.9°C, pulse 118 bpm, BP 100/68 mmHg, and respiratory rate 22/min. Her uterus is tender on palpation and her lochia is offensive. Blood cultures are drawn and a full sepsis workup is initiated. She has no known drug allergies. What is the most appropriate immediate empirical antibiotic regimen?
options: Oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg three times daily and reassess in 24 hours
IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours plus IV metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours
IV cefuroxime 1.5 g three times daily as monotherapy
Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily plus oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 7 days
IV gentamicin as monotherapy dosed by weight
explanation: Puerperal sepsis (systemic infection meeting SIRS/sepsis criteria post delivery) requires immediate empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering aerobic Gram-positives (including Streptococcus pyogenes), Gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella), and anaerobes (Bacteroides). Piperacillin-tazobactam plus metronidazole provides this breadth. Oral antibiotics are not appropriate for sepsis. Cefuroxime monotherapy lacks adequate Gram-negative and anaerobic coverage. Gentamicin monotherapy is inadequate for polymicrobial puerperal sepsis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are entirely inadequate for a patient meeting sepsis criteria with haemodynamic compromise; IV route is mandatory.
Correct — IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus metronidazole provides broad-spectrum cover for the polymicrobial aetiology of puerperal sepsis including Gram-positives, Gram-negatives, and anaerobes.
Cefuroxime monotherapy has limited anaerobic coverage and is insufficient for puerperal sepsis, which frequently involves anaerobes and E. coli.
Oral antibiotics are contraindicated in sepsis with haemodynamic instability; IV route ensures reliable bioavailability and rapid tissue levels.
Gentamicin monotherapy covers Gram-negative aerobes but lacks coverage for streptococci and anaerobes; it should be used in combination, not alone.
clinicalPearl: Puerperal sepsis requires immediate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Gram-positives, Gram-negatives, and anaerobes — piperacillin-tazobactam plus metronidazole is a common first-line combination.
keyClue: Post-caesarean fever with offensive lochia, tachycardia, and hypotension = puerperal sepsis requiring immediate IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Choosing cefuroxime alone — it lacks adequate anaerobic coverage for endometritis/puerperal sepsis.
missedConcept: Puerperal sepsis is polymicrobial; anaerobic coverage is mandatory alongside broad Gram-negative and Gram-positive cover.
redFlagReason: Tachycardia, hypotension, fever, and tachypnoea after caesarean with offensive lochia indicate sepsis requiring immediate IV antibiotics and resuscitation; delay risks septic shock.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman is scheduled for an elective lower-segment caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation for breech presentation. She has no known allergies. Her preoperative bloods are normal. A midwife asks the obstetric team when and which antibiotic should be given as surgical prophylaxis. What is the most appropriate prophylactic regimen?
options: Oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg the night before surgery
IV cefazolin 2 g given within 60 minutes before skin incision
Topical vaginal povidone-iodine only; no systemic antibiotics are required for elective caesarean
IV amoxicillin 1 g given after cord clamping to avoid foetal exposure
IV metronidazole 500 mg given 30 minutes before incision as monotherapy
explanation: Evidence from multiple RCTs and meta-analyses shows that IV cefazolin 2 g administered within 60 minutes before skin incision (i.e., before cord clamping) significantly reduces rates of endometritis, wound infection, and surgical site infection following caesarean section. Pre-incision administration is superior to post-cord-clamping timing. A single prophylactic dose is generally sufficient for elective procedures without prolonged operative time.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics the night before are not the standard prophylactic strategy for caesarean section; IV prophylaxis immediately preoperatively is required.
Correct — IV cefazolin 2 g within 60 minutes before skin incision is the evidence-based standard of care for surgical prophylaxis at caesarean section.
Topical vaginal antiseptic alone is insufficient; systemic prophylaxis significantly reduces endometritis and wound infection rates.
Post-cord-clamping timing was historically used to avoid neonatal exposure, but evidence now shows pre-incision administration is more effective and does not cause neonatal harm.
Metronidazole monotherapy provides anaerobic coverage only; the standard is a cephalosporin (cefazolin) which covers the major skin and genital tract pathogens.
clinicalPearl: Caesarean section surgical prophylaxis: IV cefazolin 2 g within 60 minutes before skin incision — pre-incision is superior to post-cord-clamping timing in reducing endometritis.
keyClue: Elective caesarean section — prophylaxis should be IV, given before incision, not after cord clamping.
commonExamTrap: Giving antibiotics after cord clamping — this practice is outdated; pre-incision administration is now standard.
missedConcept: Pre-incision timing of caesarean prophylaxis is more effective than post-cord-clamping; NICE and RCOG updated their guidance to reflect this.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman is seen in the community by her midwife on day 4 postpartum following a normal vaginal delivery. She reports feeling generally unwell with a temperature of 38.2°C, sudden onset of severe lower abdominal pain, and unusually scant and odourless lochia. Her pulse is 106 bpm. She has a 4-year-old child at home who was recently treated for a sore throat. Which organism should be most urgently considered, and what screening investigation should be prioritised?
options: Staphylococcus aureus; take a wound swab from the perineal repair
Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes); take high vaginal swab and blood cultures urgently
Escherichia coli; send urine culture and start oral trimethoprim
Chlamydia trachomatis; arrange NAAT swab at the next routine appointment
Candida albicans; prescribe topical clotrimazole for presumed thrush
explanation: The clinical picture — rapid onset severe systemic illness, early postpartum, scant (rather than heavy) odourless lochia, and contact with a household member treated for sore throat — is the classic presentation of Group A Streptococcal (GAS/Streptococcus pyogenes) puerperal sepsis. GAS produces toxins that cause rapid systemic deterioration; it is the leading cause of sepsis-related maternal death in the UK. Scant lochia (not offensive) reflects the toxin-mediated rather than tissue-pus response. High vaginal swab plus blood cultures should be taken urgently before starting high-dose IV benzylpenicillin.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Staphylococcus aureus is less commonly associated with fulminant postpartum sepsis of this speed; the household sore throat contact is the classic epidemiological clue for GAS.
Correct — GAS puerperal sepsis should be the leading concern; high vaginal swab and blood cultures must be taken urgently, followed by immediate IV benzylpenicillin without waiting for results.
E. coli UTI typically presents with dysuria, frequency, and offensive urine; it does not explain this presentation with household streptococcal contact.
Chlamydia causes PID, not acute postpartum sepsis; this presentation is far too acute and severe for chlamydial infection.
Candida causes superficial mucosal infection; it does not produce systemic sepsis with haemodynamic compromise.
clinicalPearl: Scant odourless lochia + sudden severe illness + household streptococcal contact in the early puerperium = GAS sepsis until proven otherwise; blood cultures and IV benzylpenicillin without delay.
keyClue: Child at home recently treated for sore throat — household transmission of Group A Streptococcus to the postpartum uterus is a well-recognised deadly mechanism.
commonExamTrap: Assuming foul-smelling heavy lochia is required for puerperal sepsis — GAS often causes scant, odourless lochia due to toxin-mediated rather than purulent disease.
missedConcept: GAS puerperal sepsis is the most dangerous form; scant lochia is the counterintuitive clue, and household contact with streptococcal pharyngitis is a key risk factor.
redFlagReason: Group A Streptococcal sepsis can progress to septic shock within hours; it is the leading infectious cause of maternal death in the UK — immediate blood cultures and IV benzylpenicillin are time-critical.
